danger_signals.visual_form: Tetromino pieces accumulating toward top of playfield
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game-over state; player losing control of board

danger_signals.visual_form: Gaps in horizontal lines that cannot be cleared

safety_signals.visual_form: Horizontal line completion and disappearance with visual feedback
safety_signals.meaning: Successful action; board state improvement and points earned

safety_signals.visual_form: Tetromino pieces occupying lower third of playfield
safety_signals.meaning: Player has space and control; safe operational state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Static playfield with Tetromino pieces; score/level display in UI
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Blocks fall at regular interval; player rotates and positions pieces

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Potential visual changes in piece appearance or board state per difficulty level
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Block falling speed increases; difficulty scaling observed in Dr. Mario-influenced mechanics

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Japanese version features animated title screen with visual flair; international versions use static title screen

environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version (Tetris Flash) presents animated, visually rich title; US/EU versions present plain static title — communicates intended market positioning and visual polish investment by region
Stone tablet with snake motif (difficulty level indicator in design); stone statue; UFO-esque creatures resembling Dr. Mario viruses — suggests game's hybrid nature and discarded thematic directions

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Pieces descend at gravity-affected rate; respond to player input for rotation and horizontal repositioning
Line disappears; blocks above cascade downward; score increments; visual feedback communicates success

notes: Tetris 2 (NES, 1993) is a puzzle game with minimal narrative or faction complexity. It blends Tetris block-falling mechanics with Dr. Mario-style gameplay elements. Primary visual communication is state-of-board rather than world-state or political situation. Sources document regional title screen differences and unused graphical assets (snake tablets, UFO creatures) that hint at thematic development that did not manifest in final release. Game includes debugging material and hidden sound/CHR test modes. No danger, safety, faction, or political communication systems documented beyond core puzzle mechanics.
